title: Cardiac Manifestations of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
content: Early in 2020, a controversy grew regarding the safe usage of ACE inhibitors (ACEI) and angiotensin receptor-blocking (ARB) drugs in patients infected with COVID-19. The current consensus agrees to the continued use of these medications. [27] [28] The BRACE CORONA trial found no significant difference in the number of days alive and out of hospital through 30 days among subjects receiving continuous ACEI/ARB and hospitalized for COVID-19 compared to those in whom these medicines were temporarily suspended. [29]

title: Antihypertensive Medications -- Indications -- ACE Inhibitors and ARBs
content: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are the antihypertensives of choice for patients with heart failure and chronic kidney disease. They are indicated as first-line treatment for patients with chronic kidney disease with evidence of proteinuria. JNC8 guidelines list these two classes of antihypertensive medications as first-line treatment for HTN for non-black patients, along with thiazides and CCBs. [2] Independent of their antihypertensive effect, they are proven to have a cardioprotective effect in patients with a high risk of cardiovascular disease.

title: Angiotensin Receptor Blockers and HCTZ Combination Drugs -- Indications
content: In a multinational retrospective cohort study comparing the real-world effectiveness and safety of ACE inhibitors versus ARBs as first-line treatment for hypertension, no statistically significant differences were observed between ACEIs and ARBs in primary outcomes like heart failure, acute myocardial infarction, stroke, or composite cardiovascular events. However, ARBs demonstrated a lower risk of secondary outcomes such as cough, angioedema, pancreatitis, and gastrointestinal bleeding. These findings suggest that ARBs may be preferable over ACE inhibitors due to their comparable effectiveness and potentially better safety profile in initiating treatment for hypertension. [12]

content: ARBs, as a drug class, selectively block the angiotensin II type 1 receptor and are indicated for the treatment of hypertension, congestive heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy. content: ARBs can also lower cardiovascular events and mortality in ischemic heart disease in clinical practice. However, ARB medications are inferior when compared to the use of ACE inhibitors. [2] While ACE inhibitors are generally considered first-line agents in the treatment of hypertension, they can cause a bradykinin-induced cough, deterring patients from its use. In patients who cannot tolerate ACE-inhibitor medications due to side effects, the recommendation is to change from ACE-I to ARB medication, such as olmesartan. title: ACE Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects
content: ACE metabolizes bradykinin and other local molecules. Inhibiting ACE in the lung increases the concentration of kinins, causing bronchial irritation. [39] The use of ACE inhibitors raises the risk of symptoms of airway obstruction and increases the risk of asthma or bronchial asthma worse. [23] After discontinuation of ACE inhibitor therapy, an angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) can be initiated as an alternate therapy. ARBs have a lower incidence of cough recurrence than reinitiating ACE inhibitor therapy. [40] If cough recurs on ARB therapy, switch to a different drug class entirely.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2532
content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers are frequently employed together as a combination therapy, representing a specific class of antihypertensive regimens that integrate two distinct mechanisms of action to achieve more effective blood pressure control. By pairing the renin‑angiotensin‑aldosterone system inhibition of ARBs with the vasodilatory effects of calcium channel blockers, clinicians can enhance therapeutic efficacy, reduce the likelihood of dose‑related side effects, and provide a versatile option for patients who require multiple agents to reach target blood pressure goals. This combined approach is recognized as a standard strategy in the management of hypertension and related cardiovascular conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a therapeutic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors that are administered without additional drug combinations, and benazepril, fosinopril, moexipril, perindopril, trandolapril, and zofenopril are all members of this class. Each of these agents shares the core mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to the vasoconstrictor angiotensin II, thereby lowering systemic blood pressure, reducing afterload, and providing renal and cardiovascular protection. Although they belong to the same pharmacologic group, benazepril, fosinopril, moexipril, perindopril, trandolapril, and zofenopril differ in pharmacokinetic characteristics such as half‑life, dosing frequency, and metabolic pathways, which allows clinicians to tailor ACE‑inhibitor therapy to individual patient needs, tolerability, and comorbid conditions. Recognizing that these six drugs are plain ACE inhibitors helps guide appropriate selection, monitoring, and management of patients requiring renin‑angiotensin‑aldosterone system blockade.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) are frequently used in combination therapies that are classified as “angiotensin ii receptor blockers (ARBs), combinations.” One major subgroup of these combination therapies pairs ARBs with calcium channel blockers, and this subgroup is explicitly identified as “Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers.” Another important subgroup, labeled “Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), other combinations,” also falls under the broader category of ARB combinations and includes specific agents such as the sacubitril/valsartan product. Thus, ARB combination regimens encompass both ARB‑calcium channel blocker pairings and other ARB‑based formulations, with sacubitril/valsartan representing a notable example of the latter. This hierarchical organization highlights the diverse therapeutic strategies that incorporate ARBs to enhance cardiovascular treatment outcomes.

content: Candesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, valsartan, and azilsartan medoxomil are all classified as plain angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). A specific example of such a formulation is Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers, which is classified as an ARB‑based combination therapy. Thus, ARB‑calcium channel blocker combinations are a distinct type of angiotensin ii receptor blockers (arbs), combinations, and all of these combination products fall under the larger category of agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system, guiding clinicians in selecting and understanding the hierarchy of antihypertensive therapies.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1839
content: Plain angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are a pharmacologic class of ACE‑inhibiting drugs that contain no additional active components. The class is identified by both the capitalized label “ACE INHIBITORS, PLAIN” and the lower‑case label “ace inhibitors, plain,” which refer to the same group. Members of this class include benazepril, cilazapril, enalapril, fosinopril, imidapril, lisinopril, perindopril, quinapril, ramipril, spirapril, trandolapril, and zofenopril. Within the plain ACE inhibitor subgroup, spirapril, trandolapril, and zofenopril are specifically highlighted as agents administered without additional active ingredients. All listed agents share the core mechanism of ACE inhibition and are used for the therapeutic indications typical of ACE inhibitor therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 873
content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) are frequently used in combination therapies to improve antihypertensive efficacy. One such combination, Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers, is classified as a type of “angiotensin ii receptor blockers (arbs), combinations,” indicating that it belongs to the broader group of ARB‑based combination regimens. In addition to ARB‑calcium channel blocker pairings, there are other ARB combination products, grouped under “Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), other combinations.” The fixed‑dose product nebivolol / valsartan is a member of this “other combinations” category; it pairs the beta‑blocker nebivolol with the ARB valsartan. Nebivolol itself is a component of the nebivolol / valsartan combination, illustrating how individual agents are incorporated into multi‑drug formulations. Together, these relationships highlight the structured categorization of ARB combination therapies, emphasizing the clinical strategy of pairing ARBs with agents such as calcium channel blockers or beta‑blockers to achieve more effective blood pressure control and tailored patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2951
content: Candesartan, telmisartan, and valsartan are all classified as plain angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). Each of these agents belongs to the broader drug class of ANGIOTENSIN II RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs), which act by selectively inhibiting the AT₁ receptor for angiotensin II. By blocking this receptor, these medications reduce vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation, making them effective for managing hypertension, heart failure, and protecting renal function in patients with diabetes or chronic kidney disease. Although they share the same mechanism of action and therapeutic indications, candesartan, telmisartan, and valsartan are distinct individual drugs within the ARB class, offering clinicians multiple options for individualized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2526
content: Ace inhibitors, plain constitute a pharmacologic class of angiotensin‑converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors that includes the individual agents benazepril, fosinopril, imidapril, moexipril and zofenopril. All five medications share the same mechanism of blocking the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, leading to vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and decreased blood pressure. Because they belong to the same drug class, these agents are used interchangeably in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and they exhibit similar therapeutic effects and safety considerations. Recognizing benazepril, fosinopril, imidapril, moexipril and zofenopril as members of the ace inhibitors, plain group helps clinicians select appropriate ACE‑inhibitor therapy and understand class‑wide benefits and potential adverse effects.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) plain constitute a pharmacologic class of antihypertensive agents that share the mechanism of selectively blocking the angiotensin II type 1 (AT₁) receptor, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. Candesartan, eprosartan, olmesartan medoxomil, and telmisartan are all members of the ARBs plain class. By inhibiting the effects of angiotensin II, each of these drugs helps lower blood pressure, improve cardiovascular outcomes, and is commonly used in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy. Their shared classification underscores their similar therapeutic role while allowing clinicians to choose among them based on pharmacokinetic properties, dosing convenience, and patient‑specific considerations.
